creator: swallace@reply1.sdwmail.homeunix.org
text:
>Did you try to boot last CURRENT snapshot? Maybe it is ACPI problem?
>Also, can you try attached patch?
>I slightly modified ata_phy_reset function, which is responsible
>for device detection and also I added some debug printfs.

I applied your current and previous patches to the 6.3-STABLE kernel.
Your latest patch had no apparent effect.
I did not see the sata_phy_reset messages.

Attached is a complete dmesg output from the 6.2R kernel.  It is a good boot
from a powercycle output.  After running 6.3, I reran 6.2R and tried to take
notice of differences from the scrollback (anybody know how to increase size
of scrollback since the first portion of ACPI initialization I can't check?)

The difference is at the end when it is identifying the ATA drives, instead
of the normal <ad0: messages, you get these ata0: reset messages.
It displayes these reset messages for ata0, ata1, ata2, and ata4 on a bad
boot.

On a good boot, you will see the ata_nvida_status message.  The code
indicates that "register boot attach to be run when interrupts are enabled"
via ata_delayed_attach.  My guess is ACPI is not routing the interrupts
properly and no interrupts are getting through to the ata devices (hence no
ata_nvidia_status messages).  Perhaps the code will try a ata_reinit() to
see if it can get it working one more time before giving up.

On a run with 6.3 with acpi disabled, the irqs are mapped differently.
Perhaps this accounts for it working, but doesn't explain why 6.2R works off
a power cycle.  Is there someone who is familiar with ACPI, irqs, etc who
can help determine if it is a ACPI or ATA driver problem?
With ACPI disabled, is the IRQ assignment and configuration the only thing
done differently with respect to the ata driver?  You may have fixed other
reset problems in addition to the underlying problem exhibited here.

Steven
